quote: When we see clearly, seeing is the same as stopping — we can find rest in the midst of activity.
through_line: Clear seeing and difficult seeing are the same act. What meditation reveals about the self, what an AI systems audit reveals about a DMO's content gaps, and what deep agents reveal about the limits of shallow loops — all involve the same move: looking at what is actually there, not what was expected. That is where the real work begins.

articles.title: Swarm vs. Supervisor: Multi-Agent Architecture Guide
articles.pitch: Augment Code's guide to the two dominant multi-agent topologies: swarm patterns, where routing logic is embedded in each task and agents hand off sequentially through distributed control; and supervisor patterns, where a central coordinator manages dynamic routing, ordered execution, and conflict resolution. The decision between them reduces to task interdependency. A useful capstone to the week's Craft arc, naming the architectural choice that sits above all the implementation details.

articles.title: Spring AI Agentic Patterns (Part 1): Agent Skills — Modular, Reusable Capabilities
articles.pitch: The first entry in Spring's agentic patterns series focuses on skills as the primitive unit of agent capability — modular, reusable functions that can be composed across different agent configurations. The piece makes the design case for skills-first architecture: by defining what an agent can do before defining what it is, the system stays flexible and the skill library becomes a shared resource. Connects directly to the Agent Registry / subagent-specialisation design pattern covered earlier in the week.

articles.title: Stopping & Seeing
articles.pitch: Gil Fronsdal's essay at IMC on the paired movements of meditation practice: stopping (samatha) and seeing (vipassana) are not sequential but mutually constitutive. Seeing clearly is not just a result of calm — it is what produces a different kind of calm: not the absence of experience but the capacity to be present with it without being disturbed. An accessible treatment of the non-dual quality of awareness that emerges in serious practice, written from within the tradition rather than about it.

articles.title: The Thrill of Science in 2042
articles.pitch: A speculative essay from Nautilus that projects forward from 2026 — a moment when science was under attack from denial and political turbulence — to imagine what the field looks like when it recovers its momentum. Written as a kind of time-travel dispatch, it holds the present moment's fragility and the longer arc of human curiosity in productive tension. An unusual piece of science writing: part historical, part speculative, genuinely moving.
